summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his mistaken 2006 prediction that computers had reached an inflection point in chess, then traces the remarkable progress from Deep Blue's 1997 victory to AlphaGo's 2016 defeat of Lee Sedol. He explains how the real breakthrough wasn't faster CPUs but the rise of GPUs, which are orders of magnitude faster at parallel computation and machine learning tasks. The post celebrates how consumer gaming hardware accidentally became the engine driving AI advancement, enabling sophisticated algorithmic approaches to problems far too vast for brute force. Despite the tongue-in-cheek lament about computers 'ruining' games, Atwood concludes that AlphaGo represents humanity's greatest triumph, not its defeat.
key_points: Deep Blue's specialized hardware evaluated 200 million positions per second in 1997, but modern chess engines on phones achieve grandmaster level while evaluating fewer than 20,000 positions per second - proving algorithmic efficiency matters more than raw speed
GPUs are roughly 150 times faster than CPUs at parallel computation tasks, making consumer video cards the unsung heroes of the machine learning revolution
In just 20 years, computers went from barely beating humans at Chess (problem space of 10^50) to definitively beating humans at Go (problem space of 10^170)
The cost of floating point operations dropped from $42,000 per gigaflop in 1997 to $0.08 in 2015, driven primarily by gaming video card development after 2007
AlphaGo represents a shift from brute force computation to sophisticated algorithmic approaches capable of tackling problems too vast to solve through raw processing power alone
money_quotes: In the space of just 20 years, computers went from barely beating the best humans at Chess, with a problem space of 10^50, to definitively beating the best humans at Go, with a problem space of 10^170.
Every time you see a new video card release, don't think 'slightly nicer looking games' think 'wow, hash cracking and AI just got 2× faster… again!'
If your password is only 8 characters, that's perilously close to no password at all.
Where Deep Blue was the more or less inevitable end result of brute force computation, AlphaGo is the beginning of a whole new era of sophisticated problem solving against far more enormous problems.
AlphaGo's victory is not a defeat of the human mind, but its greatest triumph.
Go has more possible moves than there are atoms in the universe.
We know that computers will continue to beat us at virtually every game we play, in the same way that dolphins will always swim faster than we do.
key_insight: The AI revolution isn't powered by specialized supercomputers but by consumer gaming hardware, making every gamer an unwitting contributor to machine learning's exponential advancement.
